From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Film genre

A **biographical film** or **biopic** ([/ˈbaɪoʊˌpɪk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"))[[1]](#cite_note-1) is a film that dramatizes the life of a real person or sometimes a group of people. Such films show the life of the person and the central character's real name is used.[[2]](#cite_note-bas2009-2) They differ from [docudrama films](/wiki/Docudrama "Docudrama") and [historical drama films](/wiki/Historical_drama "Historical drama") in that they attempt to comprehensively tell a single person's life story or at least the most historically important years of their lives.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Context

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biographical_film&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Context")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/The_Soviet_Union_1964_CPA_3130_stamp_%28Soviet_cinema_art._30th_anniversaries_of_%27Chapaev%27%2C_1934_Soviet_war_film%2C_directed_by_the_Vasilyev_brothers_for_Lenfilm%29.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Soviet_Union_1964_CPA_3130_stamp_(Soviet_cinema_art._30th_anniversaries_of_%27Chapaev%27,_1934_Soviet_war_film,_directed_by_the_Vasilyev_brothers_for_Lenfilm).jpg)

*[Chapaev](/wiki/Chapaev_(film) "Chapaev (film)")*, a 1934 biopic of Russian war hero [Vasily Chapayev](/wiki/Vasily_Chapayev "Vasily Chapayev").

Biopic scholars include George F. Custen of the [College of Staten Island](/wiki/College_of_Staten_Island "College of Staten Island") and Dennis P. Bingham of [Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis](/wiki/Indiana_University%E2%80%93Purdue_University_Indianapolis "Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis"). Custen, in *Bio/Pics: How Hollywood Constructed Public History* (1992), regards the genre as having died with the [Hollywood studio era](/wiki/Studio_system "Studio system"), and in particular, [Darryl F. Zanuck](/wiki/Darryl_F._Zanuck "Darryl F. Zanuck").[[4]](#cite_note-4) On the other hand, Bingham's 2010 study *Whose Lives Are They Anyway? The Biopic as Contemporary Film Genre*[[5]](#cite_note-5) shows how it perpetuates as a codified genre using many of the same tropes used in the studio era that has followed a similar trajectory as that shown by [Rick Altman](/wiki/Rick_Altman "Rick Altman") in his study, *Film/Genre*.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Bingham also addresses the male biopic and the female biopic as distinct genres from each other, the former generally dealing with great accomplishments, the latter generally dealing with female victimization. Ellen Cheshire's *Bio-Pics: a life in pictures* (2014) examines British/American films from the 1990s and 2000s. Each chapter reviews key films linked by profession and concludes with further viewing list.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Christopher Robé has also written on the gender norms that underlie the biopic in his article, "Taking Hollywood Back" in the 2009 issue of *Cinema Journal*.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[Roger Ebert](/wiki/Roger_Ebert "Roger Ebert") defended *[The Hurricane](/wiki/The_Hurricane_(1999_film) "The Hurricane (1999 film)")* and distortions in biographical films in general, stating "those who seek the truth about a man from the film of his life might as well seek it from his loving grandmother. ... *The Hurricane* is not a [documentary](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") but a [parable](/wiki/Parable "Parable")."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Casting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biographical_film&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Casting")]

Casting can be controversial for biographical films. Casting is often a balance between similarity in looks and ability to portray the characteristics of the person. [Anthony Hopkins](/wiki/Anthony_Hopkins "Anthony Hopkins") felt that he should not have played [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") in *[Nixon](/wiki/Nixon_(film) "Nixon (film)")* because of a lack of resemblance between the two.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The casting of [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne") as [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan") in *[The Conqueror](/wiki/The_Conqueror_(1956_film) "The Conqueror (1956 film)")* was objected to because of the American Wayne being cast as the Mongol warlord. Egyptian critics criticized the casting of [Louis Gossett Jr.](/wiki/Louis_Gossett_Jr. "Louis Gossett Jr."), an African American actor, as Egyptian president [Anwar Sadat](/wiki/Anwar_Sadat "Anwar Sadat") in the 1983 TV miniseries *[Sadat](/wiki/Sadat_(miniseries) "Sadat (miniseries)")*.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Also, some objected to the casting of [Jennifer Lopez](/wiki/Jennifer_Lopez "Jennifer Lopez") in *[Selena](/wiki/Selena_(film) "Selena (film)")* because she is a New York City native of [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico") descent while [Selena](/wiki/Selena "Selena") was [Mexican American](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans").[[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Film representations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biographical_film&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Film representations")]

Because the figures portrayed are actual people, whose actions and characteristics are known to the public (or at least historically documented), biopic roles are considered some of the most demanding of actors and actresses.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Warren Beatty](/wiki/Warren_Beatty "Warren Beatty"), [Faye Dunaway](/wiki/Faye_Dunaway "Faye Dunaway"), [Ben Kingsley](/wiki/Ben_Kingsley "Ben Kingsley"), [Johnny Depp](/wiki/Johnny_Depp "Johnny Depp"), [Jim Carrey](/wiki/Jim_Carrey "Jim Carrey"), [Jamie Foxx](/wiki/Jamie_Foxx "Jamie Foxx"), [Robert Downey Jr.](/wiki/Robert_Downey_Jr. "Robert Downey Jr."), [Brad Pitt](/wiki/Brad_Pitt "Brad Pitt"), [Emma Thompson](/wiki/Emma_Thompson "Emma Thompson"), [Tom Hanks](/wiki/Tom_Hanks "Tom Hanks"), [Eddie Redmayne](/wiki/Eddie_Redmayne "Eddie Redmayne"), and [Cillian Murphy](/wiki/Cillian_Murphy "Cillian Murphy") all gained new-found respect as dramatic actors after starring in biopics:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Beatty and Dunaway as [Clyde Barrow and Bonnie Parker](/wiki/Bonnie_and_Clyde "Bonnie and Clyde") in *[Bonnie and Clyde](/wiki/Bonnie_and_Clyde_(film) "Bonnie and Clyde (film)")* (1967), Kingsley as [Mahatma Gandhi](/wiki/Mahatma_Gandhi "Mahatma Gandhi") in *[Gandhi](/wiki/Gandhi_(film) "Gandhi (film)")* (1982), Depp as [Ed Wood](/wiki/Ed_Wood "Ed Wood") in *[Ed Wood](/wiki/Ed_Wood_(film) "Ed Wood (film)")* (1994), Carrey as [Andy Kaufman](/wiki/Andy_Kaufman "Andy Kaufman") in *[Man on the Moon](/wiki/Man_on_the_Moon_(film) "Man on the Moon (film)")* (1999), Downey as [Charlie Chaplin](/wiki/Charlie_Chaplin "Charlie Chaplin") in *[Chaplin](/wiki/Chaplin_(film) "Chaplin (film)")* (1992) and as [Lewis Strauss](/wiki/Lewis_Strauss "Lewis Strauss") in *[Oppenheimer](/wiki/Oppenheimer_(film) "Oppenheimer (film)")* (2023), Foxx as [Ray Charles](/wiki/Ray_Charles "Ray Charles") in *[Ray](/wiki/Ray_(film) "Ray (film)")* (2004), Thompson and Hanks as [P. L. Travers](/wiki/P._L._Travers "P. L. Travers") and [Walt Disney](/wiki/Walt_Disney "Walt Disney") in *[Saving Mr. Banks](/wiki/Saving_Mr._Banks "Saving Mr. Banks")* (2013), Redmayne as [Stephen Hawking](/wiki/Stephen_Hawking "Stephen Hawking") in *[The Theory of Everything](/wiki/The_Theory_of_Everything_(2014_film) "The Theory of Everything (2014 film)")* (2014), and Murphy as [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") in *[Oppenheimer](/wiki/Oppenheimer_(film) "Oppenheimer (film)")* (2023).

Some biopics purposely stretch the truth. *[Confessions of a Dangerous Mind](/wiki/Confessions_of_a_Dangerous_Mind_(film) "Confessions of a Dangerous Mind (film)")* was based on [game show](/wiki/Game_show "Game show") host [Chuck Barris](/wiki/Chuck_Barris "Chuck Barris")' widely debunked yet popular [memoir](/wiki/Memoir "Memoir") of the same name, in which he claimed to be a [CIA](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") agent.[[12]](#cite_note-12) *[Kafka](/wiki/Kafka_(film) "Kafka (film)")* incorporated both the life of author [Franz Kafka](/wiki/Franz_Kafka "Franz Kafka") and the [surreal](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") aspects of his fiction.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Errol Flynn](/wiki/Errol_Flynn "Errol Flynn") film *[They Died with Their Boots On](/wiki/They_Died_with_Their_Boots_On "They Died with Their Boots On")* tells the story of [Custer](/wiki/George_Armstrong_Custer "George Armstrong Custer") but is highly romanticized. The [Oliver Stone](/wiki/Oliver_Stone "Oliver Stone") film *[The Doors](/wiki/The_Doors_(film) "The Doors (film)")*, mainly about [Jim Morrison](/wiki/Jim_Morrison "Jim Morrison"), was highly praised for the similarities between Jim Morrison and actor [Val Kilmer](/wiki/Val_Kilmer "Val Kilmer"), look-wise and singing-wise, but fans and band members did not like the way Val Kilmer portrayed Jim Morrison,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and a few of the scenes were even completely made up.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In rare cases, sometimes called **auto biopics**,[[15]](#cite_note-15) the subject of the film plays themself. Examples include [Jackie Robinson](/wiki/Jackie_Robinson "Jackie Robinson") in *[The Jackie Robinson Story](/wiki/The_Jackie_Robinson_Story "The Jackie Robinson Story")* (1950), [Muhammad Ali](/wiki/Muhammad_Ali "Muhammad Ali") in *[The Greatest](/wiki/The_Greatest_(1977_film) "The Greatest (1977 film)")* (1977), [Audie Murphy](/wiki/Audie_Murphy "Audie Murphy") in *[To Hell and Back](/wiki/To_Hell_and_Back_(film) "To Hell and Back (film)")* (1955), [Patty Duke](/wiki/Patty_Duke "Patty Duke") in *[Call Me Anna](/wiki/Call_Me_Anna "Call Me Anna")* (1990), [Bob Mathias](/wiki/Bob_Mathias "Bob Mathias") in *[The Bob Mathias Story](/wiki/The_Bob_Mathias_Story "The Bob Mathias Story")* (1954), [Arlo Guthrie](/wiki/Arlo_Guthrie "Arlo Guthrie") in *[Alice's Restaurant](/wiki/Alice%27s_Restaurant_(film) "Alice's Restaurant (film)")* (1969), [Fantasia](/wiki/Fantasia_(singer) "Fantasia (singer)") in *[Life Is Not a Fairytale](/wiki/Life_Is_Not_a_Fairy_Tale#Film "Life Is Not a Fairy Tale")* (2006), and [Howard Stern](/wiki/Howard_Stern "Howard Stern") in *[Private Parts](/wiki/Private_Parts_(1997_film) "Private Parts (1997 film)")* (1997).

In 2018, the [musical](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") biopic *[Bohemian Rhapsody](/wiki/Bohemian_Rhapsody_(film) "Bohemian Rhapsody (film)")*, based on the life of [Queen](/wiki/Queen_(band) "Queen (band)") singer [Freddie Mercury](/wiki/Freddie_Mercury "Freddie Mercury"), became the highest-grossing biopic in history at the time.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 2023, it was surpassed by [*Oppenheimer*](/wiki/Oppenheimer_(film) "Oppenheimer (film)"), based on the life of [J. Robert Oppenheimer](/wiki/J._Robert_Oppenheimer "J. Robert Oppenheimer") and the creation of the [atomic bomb](/wiki/Nuclear_warfare "Nuclear warfare") in World War II.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
